text: If you've used Docker, you've probably typed something like this: docker run nginx.
You press enter, a few lines scroll past, and seconds later nginx is up and serving requests. This works really well — so well that most of us never look underneath.
But today, let's look underneath. What does run actually do?

Behind the scenes, docker run does three things, in order. It pulls an image. It creates a container from that image. And it starts a process inside. Pull, create, start — that's the whole plot. Let's follow it step by step.
The story takes place in two locations. The first is your own machine — Docker calls it the host. That's where you're typing, where Docker is running, and where your container is about to live.
The second is the registry: a shared library of images out on the internet. Public images like nginx live there, and unless you say otherwise, Docker looks in Docker Hub.

Step one, the pull, is simply moving the image from one place to the other. But before downloading anything, Docker checks whether the image is already on the host — maybe from an earlier run. If it is, there's nothing to do.
This time, it isn't. So Docker contacts the registry and downloads the image onto your machine, layer by layer.
When the last layer arrives, the pull is complete. The image now lives on the host.
Which raises an obvious question: what exactly did we just download? What is an image?
An image is a snapshot of a filesystem, frozen in layers. And it's completely inert — it can't do anything on its own. It's data, not a running program.
Each layer records one step of how the image was built: a base system at the bottom, then packages, then dependencies, and the application files on top.
Stacked together, those layers behave as a single, read-only filesystem. Add a little metadata — like which program to start — and that's the entire image. So far, nothing is running.
That's what step two is for: create. Docker takes the read-only image and lays one thin, writable layer on top of it. The image, plus that writable layer — that is a container.
And step three, start, launches the image's main program — for nginx, the web server — as the very first process inside the container: process ID one.
Now watch how that process uses its filesystem, because this is the elegant part. When it reads something — its configuration, say — it reads straight through to the read-only image underneath.

But when it writes something — a log file, for example — the write lands in the container's own writable layer. Even modifying an existing file copies it into that layer first. This mechanism is called copy-on-write, and it means the image underneath is never touched.
So that's the whole command. Pull the image, if you don't already have it. Create a container, by adding a writable layer. Start the main program inside. Three steps, hiding behind one word.
And understanding those steps buys us something, because now we can ask a more interesting question: what happens if you run a second container from the same image?
Here's the part I like: Docker doesn't copy the image. It creates a second container — a new process, with its own empty writable layer — and both containers read from the very same image on disk.

Let's give them names: web-1 and web-2. When web-1 writes a log file, that file lands in web-1's layer — and only there. web-2 can't see it at all. One shared image underneath, completely separate changes on top.
And that design is why containers scale so well. Add a third container, or a tenth — it's still just one image on disk. Each new container costs nothing more than its own thin, writable layer.

But this raises the deepest question in the whole story. web-1 and web-2 are, in the end, just processes — running side by side on the same operating system kernel: the host's. So what actually keeps them apart? The answer comes in two parts.
The first part is namespaces. A namespace gives a container its own private view of one kind of resource. There's one for process IDs, one for the network, one for filesystem mounts — and a few more.

Take process IDs. Inside its own namespace, each container sees its main process as PID one, and it cannot see the other container's processes at all. As far as web-1 is concerned, web-2 doesn't exist.
And the mount namespace is what lets each container treat its own small stack — the shared image plus its own writable layer — as its entire filesystem, starting at the root.
So namespaces keep containers from seeing each other. The second part of the answer is about sharing fairly: making sure no container can consume everything the host has. That job belongs to cgroups.
A cgroup — short for control group — puts a hard cap on how much CPU and memory a container can use out of the host's total.
So we can give web-2 a tighter limit, and even at full load it can never starve web-1 — or bring the host down with it.

And with that, the picture is complete. One shared kernel. One shared image. Each container isolated by its namespaces, and constrained by its cgroups. Strip away the mystery, and a container is simply an ordinary process — with a private view of the system, and a limit on what it can use.
So let's come back to where we started: docker run nginx. It still looks like one simple line — but now you can see everything behind it.
Pull the image. Create the container. Start the process. Then isolate it with namespaces, and constrain it with cgroups.
That's what happens — every single time you run a container. Thanks for watching.
